title: Remarkable representations of the 2 + 2 de sitter group
content: A paper I have been working on for a while under my professor, Peter Levay. We have been investigating the different oscillator realizations of the AdS3 space. It is not a complete paper, more like a collection of thoughts, but it gives an idea about the working habits and the mindset of theoretical physicists when they work on a problem.

title: Quantum Geometric Tensor of the SU(1,1) and SU(2) groups
content: Yet another unfinished paper, this time about the QGT of oscillator systems with different symmetries. Namely SU(1,1) and SU(2). ChatGPT gives the following definition for QGTs: The quantum geometric tensor is a mathematical object that characterizes the geometry of quantum systems, particularly in the context of entanglement. Its real and imaginary parts respectively measure the local curvature and non-local holonomy of the quantum state space. The concept of the quantum geometric tensor was introduced by Kitaev and Preskill in their 2006 paper 'Topological entanglement entropy', and has since been studied extensively in the field of quantum information and many-body physics. For example, Zhang, Chen, and Wang provided a comprehensive review of the quantum geometric tensor and its applications to the study of entanglement in pure quantum states in their 2012 paper 'Quantum geometric tensor and entanglement of pure states'. Additionally, Zhu, Lu, and Wang discussed the use of the quantum geometric tensor in the study of multipartite entangled states and its relation to the quantum Fisher information matrix in their 2014 paper 'Quantum Fisher information matrix and quantum geometric tensor for multipartite entangled states'.

title: Introduction to Machine Learning
content:
Machine learning is a topic I studied completely on my own. I tried out several forms of media including books, online courses, and YouTube videos. I found books to be the most useful, but I also think that the best way to learn about it is to try out the things you read.

A great introductory book that covers the mathematics and the scope of different ML algorithms if the The Hundred-Page Machine Learning Book" by Andriy Burkov. It covers a surprisingly broad range of topics despite its length. But the book I found to be most useful is Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras, and TensorFlow" by Aurelien Geron. It is a much lengthier piece, but as its name suggests, it covers all the necessary fields of ML including detailed coding examples. You need to have a foundation of Linear Algebra, Probability Theory and some basic Python knowledge to grasp its content but I included some great resources for these topics as well.

I recommend the following books: "Introduction to Linear Algebra" by Gilbert Strang, "Introduction to Probability" by Joseph K. Blitzstein and Jessica Hwang, and "Python for Data Analysis" by Wes McKinney. In the future I plan to upload some of my own projects to this blog so stay tuned!

title: Training and running a linear model using Scikit-Learn
content:
In the first machine learning example of this Blog I am dealing with linear models, meaning that our model explicitly assumes a linear connection between the values of the feature vectors and the labels for it.

I trained the model on the Better Life Index data to determine if money makes people happy. For example, say you want to know how happy Cypriots are, and the OECD data does not have the answer. Fortunately, you can use your model to make a good prediction: you look up Cyprus’s GDP per capita, find $37,655, and then apply your model and find that life satisfaction is likely to be somewhere around 3.75 + 37,655 × 6.78 × 10⁻⁵ = 6.30.

Then I used Matplotlib to visualize the Better Life Index data and the fit of the linear model.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression


# Download and prepare the data
data_root = "https://github.com/ageron/data/raw/main/"
lifesat = pd.read_csv(data_root + "lifesat/lifesat.csv")
X = lifesat[["GDP per capita (USD)"]].values
y = lifesat[["Life satisfaction"]].values

# Visualize the data
lifesat.plot(kind='scatter', grid=True,
             x="GDP per capita (USD)", y="Life satisfaction", label='Better Life Index data')
plt.axis([23_500, 62_500, 4, 9])
plt.title('Training and running a linear model using Scikit-Learn.py')

# Select a linear model
model = LinearRegression()

# Train the model
model.fit(X, y)

# Make a prediction for Cyprus
X_new = [[37_655.2]] # Cyprus' GDP per capita in 2020
print(model.predict(X_new)) # output: [[6.30165767]]

# Plot the data and the model
plt.plot(X, model.predict(X), color='red', label='model')
plt.plot(X_new, model.predict(X_new), 'x', color='red', label='Prediction for Cyprus (6.30165767)')
plt.legend()

# Save the plot as a PNG file
plt.savefig('Training and running a linear model using Scikit-Learn', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')

The output of this code is the following plot.



The code does the following things:

  1. Import the necessary libraries including matplotlib.pyplot, pandas, and LinearRegression from sklearn.linear_model.
  2. Define the root URL where the data is stored and download the lifesat.csv file into a pandas DataFrame named lifesat.
  3. Extract the GDP per capita (USD) and Life satisfaction columns from the lifesat DataFrame as X and y arrays respectively.
  4. Create a scatter plot of the data using the plot() method of the lifesat DataFrame.
  5. Limit the x-axis to the range [23,500, 62,500] and the y-axis to the range [4, 9].
  6. Set the title of the plot to "Training and running a linear model using Scikit-Learn.py".
  7. Create a LinearRegression model object named model.
  8. Train the model object on the X and y data using the fit() method.
  9. Generate a prediction for Cyprus' GDP per capita in 2020 and store it in X_new.
  10. Print the predicted life satisfaction score for Cyprus using the predict() method of the model object.
  11. Add the regression line to the scatter plot by plotting the predicted values using X and model.predict(X).
  12. Plot the predicted value for Cyprus using X_new and model.predict(X_new), and mark the point with a red x marker.
  13. Add a legend to the plot.
  14. Save the plot as a PNG file named "Training and running a linear model using Scikit-Learn" using the savefig() method.

I referenced the book Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras, and TensorFlow, 3rd Edition for this article.

title: TempleOS: The Third Temple of God
content:
Not too long ago I stumbled upon TempleOS by looking at different Linux distros and it got me fascinated. So I decided to give a brief introduction to this unique operation system made by a single man completely from scratch.

TempleOS is a unique and highly controversial operating system developed by Terry A. Davis. He designed TempleOS to be a platform for exploring the divine nature of computing and to create a "holy environment" where programmers could write code as an act of worship.

The name "TempleOS" comes from the concept of the third temple of God, a central idea in Jewish and Christian theology. In these belief systems, the first two temples were physical structures built by the Israelites in Jerusalem, while the third temple is considered a spiritual temple that will be built in the end times. Davis saw his creation of TempleOS as a way of fulfilling this prophecy and creating a space where programmers could commune with God through their work.

Features

Here's a brief summary of its main features

  • TempleOS is a 64-bit, x86-compatible operating system with a microkernel architecture.
  • It has a built-in programming language called HolyC, which is similar to C but with additional features and simplified syntax.
  • It has a unique command-line interface called "SparrowOS", which allows users to interact with the system using natural language commands.
  • It has a built-in text editor called "Zed", which supports syntax highlighting and autocompletion.
  • TempleOS has a powerful graphics engine that supports 640x480 resolution and 16-color graphics.

Despite its technical excellence, TempleOS has been criticized for its lack of modern features and compatibility issues with mainstream hardware. Since its last version got released in 2018 and the interface looked like this:



It had quite a lot of games in it including the so-called After Egypt, where the player can talk with God (a pseudorandom word generator), view clouds, make water, beg for meat and look at poorly drawn Moses comics. Davis thought of these comics to be some sort of a sacrifice to God, and I quote "Every male of age must make an offering three times a year".

Installation and Use

TempleOS is available for free download from the official TempleOS website. To install it, you will need to create a bootable USB drive and boot your computer from it. Detailed instructions on how to do this can be found on the TempleOS website.

Once you have installed TempleOS, you can start exploring its features and programming in HolyC. To get started, you can read the HolyC documentation that comes with the operating system or visit the TempleOS wiki, which contains a wealth of information and resources for users and developers.

The Controversy Surrounding TempleOS and Terry Davis

While TempleOS has gained a following among some programmers and computer enthusiasts, it has also generated controversy due to the views and actions of its creator, Terry A. Davis.

Davis was known for his outspoken and often offensive views, which he expressed through his personal blog and online forums. He was also known for his erratic behavior, which included public outbursts, confrontations with law enforcement, and even physical altercations.

Despite these issues, Davis continued to work on TempleOS until his untimely death in 2018. His legacy lives on through the TempleOS community, which continues to develop and improve the operating system.

References

  • The Official TempleOS Website
  • TempleOS on Wikipedia
  • "TempleOS in Qemu" by Mental Outlaw on YouTube
